title: Monogrammed Snowflake Cutout
summary: A scroll-cut plywood snowflake laid out with true six-fold symmetry — a paper-folding approach on the layout, then careful scroll-jigsaw work for the cutouts.
description:
The layout method is the whole trick to a snowflake actually looking symmetrical: mark the panel into quarters, then divide again into thirds, so the resulting lines are all 30° apart — the same geometry as folding a paper snowflake, just drawn out with a speed square or protractor instead of physically folded. Trace the pattern onto one section, then flip and repeat around the full circle so every arm matches.

The cutting is the part that rewards patience: a jigsaw fitted with a scrolling blade can follow tight curves, but interior cutouts need a starting hole drilled larger than the blade first, since a jigsaw can't plunge-cut from a solid edge. Clamp the panel securely to a sturdy table for every cut — a snowflake pattern has a lot of thin, fragile arms that can snap if the panel isn't fully supported.

tools.note: Bores the interior starting holes for the jigsaw.
A fine scrolling blade follows the tight curves of the snowflake pattern.
For laying out the 30° reference lines.

materials.name: Plywood or MDF panel, 1/2" or 3/8", 24" x 24"
Spray paint
materials.note: Recommended for the easiest, most even finish over the intricate cutout shapes.

steps.title: Lay out the reference lines
steps.body: Mark the panel as if folding it into a paper snowflake: first the quarters, then divide each quarter into thirds. Every resulting line should be 30° apart from the next — use a speed square or protractor to check.
materials: Plywood or MDF panel, 1/2" or 3/8", 24" x 24"

steps.title: Trace the pattern
steps.body: Download and print the cutout pattern at 100% scale. Place it on the panel and trace, then flip and repeat around the full circle using your reference lines until the whole snowflake outline is marked.

steps.title: Cut out the snowflake
steps.body: Clamp the panel securely to a sturdy table. Cut carefully along the traced lines with a jigsaw fitted with a scrolling blade. For any interior cutouts, drill a starting hole larger than the jigsaw blade first, since the blade can't plunge-cut from a solid surface.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand any rough edges smooth. Spray paint is recommended for the easiest, most even finish across the many small cut edges.

images.alt: Scroll-cut plywood monogrammed snowflake wall decor
